title: Three States of Your Nervous System

hook.rwenLine: Most of what you call 'feelings' are actually nervous-system states. Three of them. Knowing which one you're in changes what you can do about it.
hook.culturalNote: Polyvagal theory (Stephen Porges) gives us a useful map: regulated (rest-and-digest), activated (fight-or-flight), and shut-down (freeze). The neuroanatomy is debated; the clinical utility of the three-state model is well-established.

chunks.native: Calm, connected, present. The body's default when there's no real threat.
Mobilised for action — heart rate up, breath quick, mind racing. Fight or flight.
chunks.phonetic: Ready to do something. Sometimes useful.

chunks.native: Flat, numb, hopeless, going-through-motions. The body's emergency brake.

pattern.name: Name the state. Match the intervention.
pattern.explanation: Regulated needs nothing — enjoy it. Activated needs a long exhale or a wall to push against — somewhere for the energy to go. Shut-down needs gentle movement and warmth — never logic or pep talks. Mismatching state and intervention is why advice rarely helps.

examples.target: Activated: heart racing before a hard conversation
examples.native: Long exhale (4 in, 6 out) × 5. Then begin.

examples.target: Shut-down: can't get off the couch on Sunday afternoon
examples.native: Stand up. Walk to the kitchen. Drink water. Don't decide anything yet.

examples.target: Regulated: dinner with people you love, no agenda
examples.native: Notice. This is the state everything else is for.

pairs.right: Heart fast, breath shallow, mind racing

exercises.question: You're in shut-down on a Sunday afternoon. Someone tells you to 'just exercise, you'll feel better.' Why does that not work?

options.text: Exercise is too hard from shut-down — the gap is too big.
Exercise never works for low mood
Sunday is the wrong day to exercise

exercises.explanation: Shut-down can't access activated states directly. The ladder is shut-down → small movement → standing → walking → real activity. Skipping rungs is why pep talks fail.

exercises.prompt: Heart racing, can't sit still, brain on a loop

exercises.sentence: Activated needs somewhere for the _____ to go.

exercises.translation: Don't reach for a tool until you know what you're using it on.

exercises.question: Which of these is shut-down rather than depression?

options.text: Heavy limbs, foggy thinking, going-through-motions for an afternoon — eases when you go for a slow walk
Persistent low mood for weeks, loss of interest, sleep disrupted

exercises.explanation: Shut-down is a temporary nervous-system state, usually reversible by gentle movement. Depression is a clinical condition that needs more than a walk. If you're not sure which you're in for more than a few weeks, talk to a GP.

rwenDialogue.intro: Let's name your current state before doing anything else with it.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Right now — regulated, activated, or shut-down?

userChoices.target: Activated. My breath is shallow and I'm not staying still.
userChoices.feedback: Good. So the move is long exhales, or pushing against a wall, or a brisk walk. Energy needs a route.

userChoices.target: Shut-down. I feel heavy and I can't be bothered with this.
userChoices.feedback: Good naming. The move from shut-down isn't motivation — it's the smallest action. Stand up, get water.

userChoices.native: The state everything else is for.
userChoices.feedback: Then enjoy this lesson. The whole point of the work is more time here.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Naming is the practice. The intervention follows from the name.

correct: Somewhere for the energy to go

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Three times today — when you remember — name your state out loud or in your head: regulated, activated, or shut-down. Don't try to change it. Just name it. The naming is the practice.
mission.rwenSignoff: You can't choose what you don't see. Today you started to see.

phase8.scenario: User has just named their current state. Rwen helps them work out what intervention matches — and walks one through with them if they're activated or shut-down.
phase8.rwenRole: A calm, present companion who treats nervous-system states as information, not failures.
phase8.successCriteria: User has named state and picked one intervention to try in the next ten minutes.
